title: Digital Identity for Researchers
text: This workshop covers the essentials of managing your online presence as a researcher. We’ll go from the basics (making sure your publications are correctly attributed to you) to the more strategic (deciding when and how to engage publicly with your work).

title: Digital Identity for Researchers
text:
Structure



Part
Topic




1
Introduction: why visibility matters


2
CV and researcher identifiers


3
Digital identity and open access

title: Digital Identity for Researchers
text:
A note on priorities
Not everything here applies equally to everyone nor work for every profile. Many of the tips I provide are either based on my one experience or that of colleagues from my field and other fields.

title: Digital Identity for Researchers
text:
Materials
Slides and links referenced during the session are embedded in each section. All materials are openly available upon attribution.

title: 3. Digital Identity
text:
Making your publications openly available is both an ethical and legal imperative and a practical visibility strategy. Open papers get more reads, which could translate into more citations, and definitively more visibility.


Green OA | self-archiving a version of your paper in a repository:

Deposit a preprint (before peer review) or postprint (accepted manuscript, before publisher formatting) in a repository. With few exception, never the published version. Lets see the difference between versions with this paper.
Free, always permitted in some form, doesn’t depend on journal policy
Main repositories: DIGIBUG, Zenodo, SocArXiv, arXiv

Gold OA | publishing in an open access journal or paying an APC:

The paper is freely available from day one at the journal’s site
Not necessarily means paying to publish. Journals from institutions (e.g., all university and CSIC journals) are free to publish and open. They are subsidised.
But, does for which you do have to pay can be very expensive (APCs); check if your institution or funder has agreements (Plan S, transformative agreements)
Check journal policies at Sherpa/Romeo







Important



Check before you submit. Sherpa/Romeo tells you what version you can self-archive and under what embargo. Most journals allow green OA of the accepted manuscript after 6–12 months. Many allow it immediately.








Output type
Where to deposit
Example




Preprint / postprint
Zenodo, SocArXiv, arXiv (discipline-specific)
Preprint - Postprint


Dataset
Zenodo, OSF, institutional repository
Dataset


Code / software
GitHub + Zenodo (for DOI and citability)
Code


Teaching materials
Zenodo, institutional repository
Curso de bibliometría






Open Science Framework (OSF) is useful for managing project files, pre-registrations, and sharing materials across collaborators. It integrates with GitHub, Zenodo, and Google Drive.






Tip



Every deposited item gets a DOI from Zenodo. This makes datasets, code, and teaching materials formally citable — they can appear in your CV and your ORCID profile.

title: 3. Digital Identity
text:
Website structure and contents 🟡
An academic website gives you control over your digital identity. It is the one place where you decide what appears, in what order, and with what framing.

Do you need one?
🔴 If you are applying for positions or grants: yes.
🟡 If you have a permanent position: strongly recommended but not urgent.
🟢 If you are a PhD student with no publications yet: start simple, update as you go.

“You cannot have a digital reputation if you lack a real reputation. First publish, then make a name.”



What platform to use
Use whatever you are comfortable maintaining. Abandoned websites are worse than no website.

Quarto — if you already use R or Python, this is the smoothest option. Version-controlled, free hosting via GitHub Pages. (These materials are built with Quarto.) - Example: my website
WordPress — flexible, no coding required, some maintenance overhead. Our unit’s website
Google Sites — simplest option, functional but limited customisation. Dani’s website



Recommended structure
Mirror your CV, but with context:

About / Bio — 2–3 paragraphs. Who you are, what you work on, where you are based.
Research — current projects, interests, links to papers (link to repositories, not PDFs hosted on your site)
Publications — link to ORCID or a repository; don’t upload PDFs directly unless you have verified you can
Teaching — courses, materials (link to open repositories)
CV — a downloadable PDF, kept current







Important



Do not host PDFs of your papers directly on your website unless you have verified the publisher permits it. Link to the open repository version (Zenodo, SocArXiv) or to the publisher page with a note that a postprint is available on request.




Keep it simple and maintainable

One page per section is enough
Update it once or twice a year at minimum
A clean, navigable site with outdated content is better than an elaborate site you never touch

title: 3. Digital Identity
text:
Social media and outreach 🟢
Social media for researchers is not about vanity metrics. Used intentionally, it is a tool for finding collaborators, reaching non-specialist audiences, and staying current in your field.

Is it worth it?
Honest answer: it depends, and it is always optional. The research on whether social media activity translates to citations is mixed. What it does demonstrably do:

Connect you with researchers outside your immediate network
Reach practitioners, journalists, and policymakers who don’t read journals
Create a record of your work that is findable by non-specialists



Where to be (in 2025)
LinkedIn — the most broadly used professional network. Useful for reaching beyond academia: industry, policy, journalism. If you maintain only one social profile, make it this one.
Bluesky — the platform that has absorbed much of the academic Twitter/X community. Active communities in social sciences, open science, and bibliometrics. Good for real-time engagement with peers.
Platforms not recommended: ResearchGate is useful for discoverability but not for engagement. Academia.edu has a paywalled model that conflicts with open access principles.


A minimal, sustainable approach
You don’t need to be active everywhere. A realistic low-effort strategy:

When you publish something: post a short thread or note on LinkedIn/Bluesky with the key finding and a link to the open version
Follow conversations in your field — reading is valuable even without posting
Engage selectively — reply to things that are genuinely interesting to you







Note



Sharing a paper informally — even just posting it with a sentence about why it matters — can generate invitations to present, new collaborations, and media coverage. The barrier is lower than it seems.




What not to do

Don’t post things you wouldn’t sign with your name
Don’t mistake activity for impact
Don’t start something you won’t maintain — a dormant account with 3 posts from 2021 is not neutral







Tip



Early career researchers may benefit most from LinkedIn for job visibility. Senior researchers may find public outreach more strategic for transfer and impact narratives (relevant for ANECA CU applications and sexenios de transferencia).

title: 1. Introduction
text:
Warning



Our main work as academics is to conduct good and novel research and teach and mentor our students. Visibility is good to promote our contributions and make them valuable, and to promote our own careers, but should not be our main goal.


There is an important distinction between being the author of something and being visible as its author. In academia these two things do not automatically go together.
Furthermore, there are different types of visibility:

Visibility within academia. You can publish extensively and still be invisible because of inconsistent name signatures, common name, or constant affiliation changes. Be consistent and unique with your academic signature.

Visibility beyond academia. You can publish extensively and still be invisible beyond your own research community because you have no online presence, lack of open access or unique point from which to see your contribution.



The goal is not to be everywhere, but to be findable where it matters.

A common illustration: a researcher with an ambiguous name — compound Spanish surnames with diacritics, or a name shared with someone in another field — will have publications scattered across multiple ghost identities in Scopus or Web of Science. Every paper lost to a ghost identity is a citation that doesn’t count.
🔴 This is a must-fix, regardless of career stage.

title: Introduction
text:
Visibility vs. authorship






Warning



Our main work as academics is to conduct good and novel research and teach and mentor our students. Visibility is good to promote our contributions and make them valuable, and to promote our own careers, but should not be our main goal.


There is an important distinction between being the author of something and being visible as its author. In academia these two things do not automatically go together.
Furthermore, there are different types of visibility:

Visibility within academia. You can publish extensively and still be invisible because of inconsistent name signatures, common name, or constant affiliation changes. Be consistent and unique with your academic signature.
A common illustration: a researcher with an ambiguous name:

Compound Spanish surnames with diacritics,
A name shared with someone in another field

These cases tend to have publications scattered across multiple ghost identities in Scopus or Web of Science. Every paper lost to a ghost identity is a publication missing in your profile.







Visibility beyond academia. You can publish extensively and still be invisible beyond your own research community because you have no online presence, lack of open access or unique point from which to see your contribution.


A common illustration is a researcher with no Google Scholar profile or academic website:
Every search in Google or Google Academic yielding no results or the wrong results will send a bad message to potential stakeholders (e.g., reviewers, colleagues, non-academics, etc.).

The goal is not to be everywhere, but to be findable where it matters.

title: Introduction
text:
Different career stages, different realities
The effort and strategy needed for digital presence varies considerably:








Career stage
Key concern
Priority




PhD / Early postdoc
Getting attributed correctly from the start
ORCID, consistent signature


Postdoc / applying for positions
Demonstrating independence and output
Website, Google Scholar, LinkedIn


Permanent position (TU)
Consolidating identity, ANECA requirements
All the above + CVA narrative, all identifiers, project websites, teaching material


Senior researcher (CU)
Leadership, transfer, public engagement
Selected publications, projects, Google Scholar, team website, projects, teaching material, outreach









Note



The earlier you set this up, the less remedial work later. A consistent name signature from your very first paper saves hours of database deduplication down the line.


The time investment also differs. A PhD student can set up ORCID and Google Scholar in an afternoon. A senior researcher managing a group, grants, and teaching has to be much more selective about where to invest time in outreach or needs assistance from technical staff.

title: Introduction
text:
Types of activities and outputs
Academic output is broader than journal articles. Each type has different visibility mechanisms and different relevance depending on your field and career stage.

Research outputs

Journal articles and book chapters
Conference papers and proceedings
Preprints (arXiv, SocArXiv, EconStor, Zenodo…)
Datasets — increasingly required by funders and journals
Software and code — GitHub + Zenodo DOI for citability



Teaching materials
Often underrepresented in CVs despite significant effort. Can be shared openly via Zenodo or institutional repositories and made citable with a DOI.


Project-related outputs

Grant outcomes and deliverables
Project websites
Policy reports and briefs



Other contributions

Peer review activity (trackable via ORCID and Web of Science)
Editorial roles
Supervision (theses, TFMs)
Public outreach: blog posts, media appearances, social media threads







Tip



Key question for each output: Can someone find it, attribute it to you, and cite or reuse it? If not, there is a visibility gap worth closing.

title: CV and Researcher Identifiers
text: Here we are going to discuss two separate things.

title: 2. CV and Researcher Identifiers
text:
Academic CVs are evolving. The traditional list-of-outputs format is being complemented — and in some contexts replaced — by narrative formats that ask you to explain your contributions rather than just enumerate them.


Listing publications by Journal Impact Factor rewards volume and venue over actual contribution. Narrative CVs push back against this by asking:

What did you specifically contribute?
What is the significance of this work, beyond the journal it appeared in?
How does it connect to a coherent research trajectory?

Key readings on the debate:

Time to remodel the scientific CV
Using narrative CVs (UKRI format)
Cabezas-Clavijo, Á. (2024). Qué es un CV narrativo: 10 recomendaciones para su redacción eficiente. Infonomy, 2(2).




Since April 2024, the CVA-ANECA (Currículum Vitae Abreviado) is mandatory for all accreditation applications to PTU and CU under the ACADEMIA programme. It is built through the FECYT CVN Editor.
Key features of the CVA:

No page limit, but a maximum number of contributions per section (varies by TU vs. CU)
Narrative fields at two levels:

Global CV narrative: up to 5,000 characters — your research trajectory as a coherent story
Per-contribution narrative: up to 1,500 characters each — quality, impact, your specific role

Includes your researcher profiles: ORCID (validatable), Scopus ID, ResearcherID, and others
Docencia section requires either a Docentia report or a self-assessment (max. 5 pages)







Important



The CVA is not just an abbreviated CVN. The philosophy is different — fields that have a checkbox in the full CVN must be argued narratively in the CVA. If you fill in the full CVN and export, you will likely miss important fields. Work directly in the CVA format from the start.






Speak to the reviewer’s criteria — for TU, emphasize competence acquisition and methodological contributions; for CU, foreground leadership and independent research lines
Be specific about your role — “I led the design of the study and the analysis” is more convincing than “I contributed to…”
Contextualise impact without relying on JIF — cite usage stats, policy uptake, collaborations generated, or follow-on work that cites yours
Don’t undersell, don’t overclaim — reviewers are experts in the field
Career gaps and unconventional paths must be explained clearly (this is explicitly required)

🟡 Career-stage note: Early career applicants (TU) should focus narratives on competence development, new methods or techniques introduced, and proactivity. Senior applicants (CU) must demonstrate leadership in research direction and management.

title: CV and Researcher Identifiers
text:
ORCID
ORCID (Open Researcher and Contributor ID) is a persistent digital identifier that distinguishes you from every other researcher. It is now mandatory in most EU funding calls (Horizon Europe, ERC, MSCA) and required in the ANECA CVA.

What to do

Register at orcid.org — free, takes 5 minutes
Make your profile public (at least the works list)
Connect external sources: Scopus, Web of Science, Crossref, DataCite — this auto-imports your publications
Use it consistently: add your ORCID iD to every journal submission, grant application, and institutional profile



What ORCID gives you

Single persistent identifier that follows you across institutions and name changes
Interoperability: data flows between ORCID and funding agencies, publishers, and institutional systems
A BibTeX export of your works for use elsewhere
Peer review tracking (via Crossref)







Tip



ORCID is the one identifier that travels with you. Scopus and Web of Science IDs are database-specific. If you only set up one thing today, make it ORCID.

title: CV and Researcher Identifiers
text:
Scopus Author ID
Scopus automatically assigns an Author ID based on publication metadata. The problem: if your name appears inconsistently across papers, you may have multiple IDs, or share one with someone else.

What to do

Search for yourself at scopus.com using author:'your name'
Check whether all your publications are under a single profile
If not: use the Author Feedback Wizard (requires institutional access) to merge or correct profiles
Add your ORCID to link both systems
Check author-level metrics (h-index, citation counts, document counts) and understand what they do and don’t mean







Note



Scopus coverage is strong in natural sciences, medicine, and social sciences, but weaker in humanities and some regional literature. Know your field’s coverage before relying heavily on Scopus metrics.

title: CV and Researcher Identifiers
text:
Researcher ID (Web of Science)
Web of Science has its own researcher profile system, now integrated into the Web of Science platform.

What to do

Create a profile at webofscience.com
Claim your publications (use the search and add manually if needed)
Review your Beamplot — a visualisation of your citation profile over time
Link to your ORCID
Log peer review activity — Web of Science tracks this and it feeds into your ORCID profile







Note



Web of Science coverage skews towards high-impact international journals. It is the database behind the sexenios evaluation in Spain, so keeping this profile clean matters for CNEAI applications.

title: CV and Researcher Identifiers
text:
Google Scholar
Google Scholar is the most inclusive database — it indexes conference papers, theses, preprints, grey literature, and books that Scopus and WoS miss. It is also the most visible to non-specialists.

What to do

Create a Google Scholar profile
Verify it with your institutional email and make it public
Set up email alerts for new citations
Manage automatic updates: Scholar will suggest new papers — review them, as false positives do appear



Caveats

No quality control: Google Scholar can include duplicates, erroneous citations, and grey literature
Metrics (h-index, i10-index) are inflated compared to Scopus/WoS
Useful for visibility and self-monitoring, complements but does not replace Scopus/WoS for formal evaluation in Spain







Tip



Essential for early career researchers who want maximum visibility, especially in fields with significant conference and preprint culture. Less critical if your field is well-covered by Scopus/WoS and your primary audience is specialists.

title: CV and Researcher Identifiers
text:
Narrative CVs
There was a time when academic Cvs in Spain where characterised by their lack of synthesis: the longer the better. In recent years this has changed. The Coalition for the Reform of Research Assessment has now been implanted in many national research evaluation systems all over Europe and beyond.





Spain is one of the countries in which this has had a greater impact, with ANECA, CRUE and CSIC leading the reforms in Spain.






Note



Want to learn more about CoARA?

Check out its guiding principles
Visit ANECA’s website devoted to CoARA



Among other things, this introduces too major changes on how we present ourselves to funders:

JIFs and citations are not enough. CoARA is against the abuse of Journal Impact Factors and encourages not to use them. We should add further contextual arguments when defending our papers:

Contribution to the field
Societal impact
Novelty
Funding

Narratives are welcome! This means making the case for how and why our contributions are meaningful and contextualising them within our own research agenda.


The main goal is to move from the traditional list-of-outputs format to narrative formats that ask you to explain your contributions rather than just enumerate them.


Why the shift?
Listing publications by Journal Impact Factor rewards volume and venue over actual contribution. Narrative CVs push back against this by asking:

What did you specifically contribute?
What is the significance of this work, beyond the journal it appeared in?
How does it connect to a coherent research trajectory?

Key readings on the debate:

Time to remodel the scientific CV
Using narrative CVs (UKRI format)



The ANECA CVA: what it requires
Since April 2024, the CVA-ANECA (Currículum Vitae Abreviado) is mandatory for all accreditation applications to PTU and CU under the ACADEMIA programme. It is built through the FECYT CVN Editor.
Key features of the CVA:

No page limit, but a maximum number of contributions per section (varies by TU vs. CU)
Narrative fields at two levels:

Global CV narrative: up to 5,000 characters: your research trajectory as a coherent story
Per-contribution narrative: up to 1,500 characters each: quality, impact, your specific role

Includes your researcher profiles: ORCID, Scopus ID, ResearcherID, and others
Docencia section requires either a Docentia report or a self-assessment (max. 5 pages)







Important



The CVA is not just an abbreviated CVN. The philosophy is different: fields that have a checkbox in the full CVN must be argued narratively in the CVA. If you fill in the full CVN and export, you will likely miss important fields. Work directly in the CVA format from the start.




Tips for writing effective narratives

Speak to the reviewer’s criteria

For EARLY CAREER APPLICANTS focus narratives on competence development, new methods or techniques introduced, and proactivity.
For TITULAR, emphasize competence acquisition and methodological contributions
For CATEDRATICO, foreground leadership and independent research lines, demonstrate leadership in research direction and management.

Be specific about your role: “I led the design of the study and the analysis” is more convincing than “I contributed to…”
Contextualise impact without relying on JIF: cite usage stats, policy uptake, collaborations generated, or follow-on work that cites yours
Don’t undersell, don’t overclaim: reviewers are experts in the field
Career gaps and unconventional paths must be explained clearly (this is explicitly required)
